---
title: "Personalization as Quiet Information Control"
subtitle: "Internet Censorship and Online Speech"
author: "Nick Feamster · University of Chicago"
header-includes: |
  <style>
    /* No ALL-CAPS anywhere */
    .reveal h1, .reveal h2, .reveal h3, .reveal h4, .reveal h5, .reveal h6,
    .reveal .title, .reveal .subtitle,
    section.title-slide h1, section.title-slide .subtitle {
      text-transform: none !important;
      letter-spacing: normal !important;
    }
    /* Uniform title sizes (absolute px — unaffected by .smaller) */
    .reveal h1, .reveal .smaller h1, .reveal section.smaller h1 { font-size: 46px !important; }
    .reveal h2, .reveal .smaller h2, .reveal section.smaller h2 { font-size: 38px !important; }
    .reveal h3, .reveal .smaller h3, .reveal section.smaller h3 { font-size: 30px !important; }
    section.title-slide h1 { font-size: 56px !important; }
    section.title-slide .subtitle { font-size: 32px !important; }
    /* Body sizing (a bit bigger than before) */
    .reveal { font-size: 30px; }
    .reveal .smaller, .reveal section.smaller { font-size: 0.78em; }
    .reveal ul, .reveal ol, .reveal p, .reveal blockquote { font-size: 0.88em; }
    .reveal li { line-height: 1.3; margin-bottom: 0.15em; }
    .reveal table { font-size: 0.72em; }
    .reveal pre, .reveal code { font-size: 0.72em; }
  </style>
---

## "A Squirrel Dying in Front of Your House" {.center}

> "A squirrel dying in front of your house may be more relevant to your interests
> right now than people dying in Africa."

The line (attributed to Mark Zuckerberg in Eli Pariser's *The Filter Bubble*, 2011)
is the whole problem in one sentence: **relevance is decided for you, by an
algorithm, and you never see what it left out.**

::: {.notes}
Open with the squirrel line. It captures the engagement logic of personalization:
the feed optimizes for what holds *your* attention, not for what you ought to see.
Pariser coined "filter bubble" around this. The pedagogical move for today: treat
personalization not as a feature but as a **form of information control** — one that
works through omission and amplification rather than a visible block. See
censorship-book Ch. 3 Platform Controls, §3.7.
:::

# Personalization Is a Tax on Access {.center}

## Not All Control Is Overt

Most of this course is about visible control — blocks, takedowns, shutdowns. **Personalization
is different:** it shapes what you see *quietly*, in the background.

- It optimizes for **engagement**, but in doing so it decides **visibility** and **relevance**
- It is **rarely transparent** — you can't see the results it withheld
- It works through **omission and amplification**, not a "BLOCKED" page

This is a textbook case of **friction**: not a wall, but a quiet **tax on access** to
the information you didn't get recommended. The driver is **economic**: an ad-funded
feed is paid to **maximize attention**, and reinforcing content holds attention best —
so bubble formation is a *business model*, not a bug *(see Ch. 4, Economic Controls)*.

::: {.notes}
Tie back to the Roberts taxonomy from the Intro deck: Fear / Friction / Flooding.
Personalization is friction (content is *harder* to reach because it's buried or never
surfaced) and it sets up flooding (data voids, later). The key analytic claim from the
book: personalization is information control *because* the user can't observe what was
filtered. No backlash, no martyr, full deniability — "it's just the algorithm."

Item 2 cross-link: name the *root cause* out loud. The feed is optimized for engagement
because engagement is what's monetized — every reinforcing recommendation is an attention
trade the platform is economically rewarded for. That's why "just turn off the algorithm"
is naive: the incentive, not the code, is the problem. This is the hand-off to the
economic-controls material in Ch. 4 — don't develop it here, just plant the pointer.
:::

## Personalization Is Ubiquitous

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
- **Search** results
- **Social feeds** (ranking, "For You")
- **News** portals and aggregators
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
- **Shopping** — goods and services
- **Media** — music, movies, video
- **Maps / local** results
:::
:::

Anywhere you get a **personalized recommendation**, ask: *what results are being
filtered out, and would I ever know?*

::: {.notes}
The book's point: the same mechanism spans every content type. Most of it feels benign
or even helpful — an Old Navy search in Santa Barbara vs. Amherst returns your local
store. That's the wedge: the *same* machinery that gives you the nearest store can give
you a different reality on "Egypt," "vaccines," or a ballot measure. Benign and
political personalization are the same code path.
:::

## From Benign to Political

A search for **Old Navy** returning your nearest store is harmless personalization.

A search for **"Egypt"** that returns *tourism* for one user and *current events /
protests* for another is **not** — the same query yields different realities.

::: {.vignette}
The boundary between **personalization** and **censorship** is blurry by design. In the
best case you merely miss a relevant result. In the worst case, relevant information
becomes **practically undiscoverable** — and you have no way to know it's missing.
:::

::: {.notes}
This is the core conceptual slide. Personalization sits on a continuum with censorship;
there's no bright line. The censor doesn't have to remove content — making it
un-surface for the people who'd care is enough. Note that the *user's lack of awareness*
is what makes it powerful: undocumented friction is the most effective friction.
:::

## Filter Bubbles and Echo Chambers {.smaller}

**Eli Pariser (2011):** algorithmic personalization can isolate users in ideological
**echo chambers** — you see more of what you already agree with, less of what challenges
you, in a self-reinforcing **feedback loop**.

But the story has gotten **more complicated**:

- Newer research finds users *are* often exposed to diverse perspectives —
  and then **selectively engage** with the reinforcing ones
- The bubble may be as much **human choice** as **algorithmic enclosure**

::: {.notes}
Be honest with students that the strong 2011 filter-bubble thesis has been partly walked
back. The mechanism is real, but "the algorithm traps you" is too clean. Selective
exposure (a human bias) and algorithmic curation interact. The book flags this explicitly
— don't oversell Pariser. The interesting research question is disentangling the two.
:::

## The Bubble Has Moved: Passive Signals {.smaller}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
**TikTok's "For You" page** curates on **passive behavioral signals** — watch time,
scroll speed, rewatches — *not* declared interests or your social graph.

- Highly personalized, highly **opaque**
- Users can be pulled toward reinforcing or **radicalizing** content without
  understanding why
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
**Closed platforms** (WhatsApp, Telegram) build bubbles a different way: through
**group membership**, with content **forwarded peer-to-peer**, unlabeled and
unmoderated — potent around **elections and crises**.
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Two updates the book makes to the 2011 picture. (1) Engagement-optimized feeds like
TikTok don't need you to declare anything — passive signals are enough, and the system
can't really explain itself. (2) In much of the world the "bubble" isn't algorithmic at
all; it's a WhatsApp/Telegram group with no external moderation. Both matter for
elections. This sets up the regulatory vignette.
:::

## Weak Signals, Strong Inferences {.smaller}

Recommenders don't need a political signal to place you politically. They
infer identity from **many weakly correlated traces** — a movie you liked,
a hobby, a genre — and treat those as **strong evidence** for cluster
placement.

- **Narayanan (Knight Inst., 2023):** engagement-optimized systems recruit
  *any* signal correlated with watch time, follows, or shares — regardless
  of topic
- Cross-domain correlations become an **engine** for moving users between
  bubbles on inputs that look apolitical
- **Ribeiro et al. (FAccT 2020)** trace user migration across YouTube from
  mainstream commentary → Alt-lite / IDW → Alt-right — a migration pattern
  consistent with the mechanism (though the YouTube empirical picture
  remains contested)

::: {.vignette}
Illustration (from a class discussion): a fandom survey found dislike of
*The Last Jedi* correlated with more conservative and anti-progressive
attitudes. The recommender doesn't need to know your politics — knowing
your movie opinion is already a probabilistic handle. *(Non-peer-reviewed
fandom survey; use it only for intuition on the mechanism.)*
:::

::: {.notes}
This is the *mechanism* behind the TikTok audit on the previous slide. The
partisan skew Ibrahim et al. observed almost certainly wasn't an explicit
ideological rule — it's the downstream consequence of many small signals
being combined into a strong inference about identity and attention.
Narayanan (2023) is the general primer: recommenders learn latent user
representations from behavioral traces, and whatever signal predicts the
optimization target gets recruited, topically related or not. Ribeiro et
al. supply the empirical migration picture on YouTube; be candid that this
picture is contested (Ledwich & Zaitsev found the opposite with anonymous
seeds), which is itself the point: mechanism strength depends on how much
personalization signal the system already has. The Star Wars vignette is
an illustration to make the point memorable; do not overclaim from it.
<!-- CITE TODO: no clean peer-reviewed source proves taste-signal →
political-cluster migration end-to-end; keep the framing as an inference
from mechanism + observed migration, not as a proven finding. -->
:::

## The "Rabbit Hole": Contested Evidence {.smaller}

Does the **YouTube** recommender *push* users toward extremism? The research is
genuinely **mixed** — a cautionary tale about over-reading any single audit.

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Evidence for a pull**

- Watch-time optimization can *learn* to surface radicalizing content
- A PNAS sock-puppet audit (Haroon et al., 2023) found recommendations grow
  **more congenial** and **more problematic** deeper in the trail
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Evidence against**

- Other audits find the algorithm favors **mainstream** sources and even
  **dampens** extremist content
- "Rabbit holes" are often driven by **user choice / subscriptions**, not the
  feed alone
:::
:::

The honest read: **congenial, yes; an automatic extremism escalator, not shown.**

::: {.notes}
This is the item-4 slide and it pairs with the TikTok passive-signal slide before it.
The teaching goal is epistemic humility, not a verdict. The strong "YouTube radicalizes
people" claim (popular ~2018-2019, e.g. the Tufekci "rabbit hole" framing) has been
substantially complicated. Verified anchors: Haroon et al., PNAS 2023, "Auditing
YouTube's recommendation system..." (100k sock puppets) found congenial and increasingly
*problematic* recommendations deeper in the trail, but did NOT find meaningful increases
in ideological *extremity*. Counter-work (e.g. Hosseinmardi et al.; naturalistic PNAS
2022/2024 studies) finds limited polarization effects and a strong role for subscriptions
and off-platform choices. Why so mixed? The algorithm changes constantly and outsiders
can't see it — the same measurement problem as the next slide. Don't let students walk
away thinking the science is settled in either direction.
Sources: pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.2213020120; pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.2318127122.
:::

## 2025: Regulators Demand the Algorithm Open Up

::: {.vignette}
On **October 24, 2025**, the **European Commission** preliminarily found **TikTok and
Meta** in breach of the **Digital Services Act** for failing to give vetted researchers
adequate access to platform data (DSA **Article 40**) — the access needed to study how
recommender systems shape what users see. A confirmed finding can trigger fines up to
**6% of global annual turnover**; the delegated act on researcher data access took
effect **October 29, 2025**. *(Source: European Commission press release IP/25/2503.)*
:::

The fight is now explicitly about **algorithmic transparency** — can outsiders even
*measure* the bubble?

**US, a different lever:** New York's **SAFE for Kids Act** lets minors be shown
**algorithmic feeds only with parental consent** (default to chronological); proposed
rules were out for comment through **Dec. 2025**. A separate NY law (signed **Dec. 2025**)
mandates **warning labels** on addictive-feed features for under-18 users.

::: {.notes}
This is the freshest verified hook (swap annually — see coverage-notes). Teaching point:
the policy response to personalization-as-control is to force *measurability*. You can't
regulate what you can't observe, and platforms tightly control access to exactly the data
that would let researchers audit recommendation. The DSA Article 40 data-access regime is
the live battleground. This connects forward to the Legal chapter (Ch. 4, DSA).

Item-5 balance (don't let the deck read EU-only): the US has no DSA-style transparency
mandate, but states are regulating the *feed itself*. Verified: NY's SAFE for Kids Act
(signed 2024) restricts "addictive" algorithmic feeds for minors — under-18s get a
non-personalized (e.g. chronological) feed unless a parent consents; AG Letitia James'
proposed rules were in public comment through Dec. 1, 2025 and take effect 180 days after
final rules. A companion NY law (S4505/A5346, signed Dec. 2025) requires Surgeon-General-
style warning labels on autoplay/infinite-scroll/algorithmic-feed features for minors.
Contrast the levers: EU = transparency/data-access (audit the algorithm); US state laws =
design mandates (restrict the algorithm for kids). Both face First Amendment / litigation
headwinds in the US — flag that the status may move; see coverage-notes.
Sources: ag.ny.gov SAFE for Kids NPRM (2025); nysenate.gov S4505/A5346 (Dec. 2025).
:::

# Can We Measure the Bubble? {.center}

## Auditing Recommendation Systems Is Hard

Platforms tightly control access to algorithmic transparency and usage data. Researchers
improvise:

- **Sock-puppet accounts** — synthetic profiles to probe what each "user" is shown
- **Adversarial queries** — vary the input to expose differences
- **Data donations** — volunteers contribute their own results

The recurring obstacle: **the platform is the thing you're trying to audit**, and it
controls the data.

::: {.notes}
Frame measurement as an adversarial, methods-heavy problem — the same flavor as Ch. 5.
The DSA fight (previous slide) is precisely about whether the sock-puppet / data-donation
approach can be replaced by sanctioned data access. Now we'll look at one concrete audit
method from our own work: Bobble.
:::

## Bobble: Seeing What Was Filtered {.smaller}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="48%"}
A **browser plugin** (Bobble, Xing et al., PAM 2014). When a user runs a Google search,
Bobble also runs the **same query** from distributed servers worldwide, **as different
users, with different histories**.

It then highlights results that *would have appeared* for someone else — the
**results you didn't see**.
:::
::: {.column width="52%"}
![Bobble UI: a normal results page, with the filtered-out results (issued from other vantage points) highlighted in yellow.](images/slide024_img040.png)
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Walk through the design: execute the query from many vantage points (locations,
profiles, logged-in vs. out), then diff the result sets. The UI surfaces, in yellow, the
results filtered from *this* user but visible elsewhere. The method asks three questions:
what shows up on page one, where it ranks, and — when something doesn't appear — why.
This is the concrete instantiation of "what results am I not seeing?"
:::

## What Causes the Differences? {.smaller}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Static features**

- Location (IP address)
- Browser, OS
- Logged in vs. out
- Gender, age
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Historical features**

- Search history
- Click history
- Browsing history
:::
:::

Disentangling **which** factor drives an inconsistency is the hard part — attribution,
not just detection.

::: {.notes}
The study (≈200 users, 75,000+ queries, nine months in 2012) tried to attribute
inconsistencies to causes. The two big buckets: *who you are* (profile) and *where you
are* (geography). Establishing causality is genuinely hard — these factors covary. Caveat
the students: this is 2012-era data; the specific numbers likely look different today,
but the method and the phenomenon persist.
:::

## Bobble Findings: Geography Dominates {.smaller}

From the 2012 study (caveat: a decade old, worth revisiting):

- **>85%** of queries had a result **absent** from the user's set but in the **top 10**
  for some other geography
- **Geography** explained more inconsistency than **profile**
- Logged-in vs. logged-out often changed the **order** of results (incl. what's on
  page one) more than the **set**

::: {.vignette}
**"The Internet" is not one thing.** Where you search can change what is even
*discoverable* — and rank changes alone decide what most users ever click.
:::

**Does it still replicate?** Later audits find the *magnitude* of profile
personalization is **smaller** than the 2011-era hype: Hannak et al. measured **~12%**
of results differing, driven mainly by **login + location** — and donated-search studies
put the room for a profile "bubble" at **under ~2 of 10** results. The durable finding is
**geography/location**, not a personal ideological enclosure.

::: {.notes}
Headline: geography was the bigger lever, and even when the *set* of results was stable,
*ranking* shifted — and ranking is destiny, since almost no one leaves page one. So
"censorship by ranking" doesn't require removing anything. Stress the caveat that this is
2012 data; Google has changed a lot.

Item-3 strengthening (the deck's weakest dated figure): rather than just caveat "it's
old," anchor it to verified follow-up work so students see where the field landed.
Hannak et al. ("Measuring Personalization of Web Search") found on average ~11.7% of
results differ from personalization, dominated by being logged in and by IP/location, not
by deep profile history. Robertson et al. (2018) and large donated-search analyses
similarly find the space for a profile-driven "filter bubble" is small (often <2 results
of 10 for person queries). Net: the *geography* result is the robust one; the strong
"the algorithm builds you a personal ideological bubble" reading is weak in search
(stronger on engagement feeds — see the TikTok/YouTube slides). This keeps the >85%
top-10-by-geography number honest by pairing it with the modern, smaller profile-effect
estimates. Sources: Hannak et al. (arXiv:1706.05011 / cbw.sh); donated-search audits
(arXiv:1812.10943).
:::

# Personalization Can Be Attacked {.center}

## Adversarial Personalization {.smaller}

If you can **pollute a user's profile** (their inferred history), you can move content
**into or out of** their results — **without editing a single underlying page.**

- Search engines deliver **inconsistent** results across users, and to the *same* user
  over time
- An attacker who shapes those signals can **sway opinion** on products, candidates,
  or ballot measures

*"Take This Personally: Pollution Attacks on Personalized Services"* (Xing et al.,
USENIX Security 2013) demonstrated this in practice.

::: {.notes}
Key escalation: personalization isn't just a passive side effect — it's an *attack
surface*. The corpus is untouched; the attacker manipulates the *profile* (or the
signals the recommender reads) so a target sees different results. This is information
control without takedowns — perfectly deniable. Note the same observed inconsistency
(CA vs. GA results on a ballot proposition) can be a research finding *or* a weapon.
:::

## Data Voids {.smaller}

A **data void** is a query that returns **little authoritative content** — an obscure
health term, a freshly coined phrase, a breaking-news topic.

- Coined by **Golebiewski & boyd** (Data & Society, 2018)
- Bad actors **fill the void** with biased or false material, using **adversarial SEO**:
  keyword stuffing, coordinated backlinking, micro-site cloning
- Especially dangerous during **breaking news** and **public-health crises**, before
  authoritative sources catch up

::: {.notes}
Data voids are the flip side of the bubble: instead of burying content, you *plant* it
where nothing trustworthy exists yet. Coin a term ("crisis actor," an invented
syndrome), then own the search results for it. This is flooding/manipulation in the
Roberts sense. A 2025 arXiv study (Data Voids and Warning Banners on Google Search)
found Google shows a warning banner for only ~1% of queries — voids largely go
unflagged. Generative-AI chatbots reproduce some disinformation for the same reason:
they answer into voids — developed on the next slide.
:::

## Chatbots: The New Surface for Voids {.smaller}

Generative-AI assistants are becoming the **default answer layer** — and they
**answer into data voids**, where little authoritative content exists to ground them.

- **NewsGuard (Mar. 2025):** audited **10 leading chatbots** (ChatGPT, Gemini,
  Copilot, Grok, Perplexity, Meta AI, others); they repeated false claims seeded by
  the pro-Kremlin **"Pravda" network** about **one-third** of the time
- The seeded sites exist largely **to be scraped** by models, not read by humans —
  void-filling aimed at the **training/retrieval pipeline**

::: {.vignette}
**Manipulation or just gaps?** A *HKS Misinformation Review* (2025) reanalysis argues the
chatbots cite these sources mostly on **narrow, obscure queries** — i.e., the references
reflect **data voids**, not successful "LLM grooming." The mechanism is the same one
we've seen all lecture: **own the void, own the answer.**
:::

::: {.notes}
Item-1 slide. Frame chatbots as the *newest* personalization/curation surface: instead of
ranking ten links, the model returns one synthesized answer — so the void problem gets
worse, because there's no result list to inspect and no obvious "I didn't see X." Verified
anchors: NewsGuard's March 2025 audit of 10 chatbots found ~33% repetition of Pravda-
network falsehoods (france24/axios coverage; NewsGuard special report). The crucial nuance
— and why the vignette is a *question* — is the Harvard Kennedy School Misinformation
Review 2025 piece ("LLMs grooming or data voids?"), which argues the chatbots surface
these sources mainly on narrow/obscure prompts, consistent with data voids rather than
deliberate manipulation. Either way it lands the section thesis: a chatbot answering into
a void reproduces whatever filled it. Tie back to the Golebiewski & boyd data-void slide
just before this. Keep it honest: don't assert "AI is groomed by Russia" as settled.
:::

## Data Voids in News Portals {.smaller}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
A Google News study (≈3,800 English sources, 5 months, 22 countries) found
personalization narrows the news you see:

- **20%** of sources produced **80%** of articles in some countries
- Source counts ranged from **~1,500 (Israel)** to **~200 (New Zealand)**
- Some queries returned **no results** in one country but stories in another — i.e.,
  **filtering**
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
![Distinct news sources by country (and sources contributing >5, >10, >15, >20 articles). Most diversity collapses onto a few sources.](images/slide039_img043.png)
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
This is the data-void / filter-bubble phenomenon in news, with real numbers. The plot:
the tall black bars are *all* sources, but the colored bars (sources with more than 5,
10, 15, 20 articles) are much shorter — a few sources dominate the feed. Articles-per-
source ranged ~130:1 (NZ, Switzerland — low diversity) to ~35:1 (US — more diverse).
Some countries even saw complete filtering of certain queries. "The Internet looks
very different to different people" — empirically.
:::

## When the Fix Makes It Worse: Algospeak {.smaller}

Moderation is supposed to *counter* the bubble. Instead it can **deepen**
it, by pushing creators to adapt.

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="52%"}
- Platforms throttle recommendations on flagged topics (violence,
  self-harm, sexuality, contested political terms)
- Creators learn the trigger words and **substitute**: "unalive" for kill,
  "SA" for sexual assault, "spicy eggplant" for vibrator
- Popularized as **algospeak** by Taylor Lorenz (*Washington Post*, Apr.
  2022); Klug, Steen & Yurechko (WWW '23) and Steen, Yurechko & Klug
  (*Social Media + Society* 2023) confirm the practice is deliberate and
  strategic
- When no substitution works, creators **stop making content on the topic
  at all**
:::
::: {.column width="48%"}
**Not just words — behavior.** Broader creator-adaptation literature:

- **Cotter (2019)** — "playing the visibility game" with an opaque ranker
- **Karizat et al. (2021)** — folk theories of an "identity strainer"
  algorithm; users modify behavior accordingly
- **DeVito (2022)** — transfeminine TikTok creators sometimes elect *not
  to post* rather than risk demotion
- **Duffy & Meisner (2023)** — algorithmic (in)visibility as platform
  governance at the margins
:::
:::

::: {.vignette}
**Second-order filter bubble.** The engagement bubble (what gets ranked up)
is compounded by a **self-censorship bubble** (what gets made in the first
place). Moderation intended as a countermeasure produces a **deeper** filter
bubble than the one it was designed to correct.
:::

::: {.notes}
This is the second big update to the 2011 Pariser picture: treat creators as
adaptive agents, not passive suppliers. Once you do, the platform's
moderation choices reshape the *supply side* of the content catalog, not
just its ranking. Roberts' *Behind the Screen* (Yale, 2019) provides the
upstream infrastructure story — opaque, contractor-driven enforcement
decisions that creators can only reverse-engineer — which is why the observed
response is linguistic avoidance rather than appeal. Jake's framing from
lecture: "moderation intended as a countermeasure produces a deeper filter
bubble." Land this before Takeaways so students see friction operating on
*two* layers: what is surfaced, and what is produced at all.
:::

## Takeaways {.smaller}

- Personalization is **quiet information control** — it shapes what you see by
  **omission and amplification**, not visible takedowns, which makes it **hard to detect
  and hard to regulate**
- Personalization can be **attacked** — pollute a profile or exploit a **data void** and
  you move content without editing the corpus
- Empirical audits find **less source diversity than users assume**, varying sharply by
  **country and profile** — *"the Internet" is not one thing*

::: {.notes}
These are the book's three section takeaways verbatim in spirit. Land the synthesis:
personalization is friction + flooding combined, it's deniable, it's an attack surface,
and the policy frontier (DSA Article 40) is about making it *measurable* at all.
:::

# Where This Connects {.center}

Personalization is the **subtle** end of platform control — moderation and information
operations are the overt ends.

Next: how all of this is **governed and contested** — platform transparency law
(EU **DSA**) in **Ch. 4 (Legal & Economic Controls)**.

*See censorship-book Ch. 3 Platform Controls, §3.7 (Personalization).*

::: {.notes}
Closing pointer. Locate today in the larger map: Ch. 3 covers content moderation
(overt), information operations (flooding), and personalization (quiet friction).
Personalization is the hardest to see and therefore the hardest to regulate — which is
exactly why the measurement and transparency-law threads (Ch. 5, Ch. 4) matter. End by
reminding them of the squirrel.
:::
